title: Can You Stop a Kundalini Awakening Once It Starts?
meta_description: You're here, aren't you? Probably 3 AM, staring at your ceiling, scrolling through forums, searching for answers to something that feels like it's tearing
body:
Can You Stop a Kundalini Awakening Once It Starts?

You're here, aren't you? Probably 3 AM, staring at your ceiling, scrolling through forums, searching for answers to something that feels like it's tearing you apart from the inside out. You're asking if you can stop it. That's the question that screams in your head when you're overwhelmed, when the energy is too much, when you feel like you're losing your mind. I know that feeling. I've been there. The short, brutal answer? No. You can't stop it.

I know that's not what you want to hear. Trust me, I get it. I spent months, years even, wishing I could just turn it off, go back to normal. Whatever normal was before this kicked in. You're not alone in feeling this desperate desire for an off switch. This isn't a lightbulb you can just flick. Once this energy, whatever you want to call it - kundalini, spiritual awakening, dark night of the soul - once it's really moving, it's like a river that's burst its banks. You can't just put the water back in the riverbed. It's gotta flow, it's gotta find its new course. And you, my friend, are caught in that current.

Here's the thing: trying to stop it is often what makes it worse. You're fighting against something incredibly powerful, something that's trying to clean house, to reorganize you from the cellular level up. Imagine trying to hold back a tsunami with your bare hands. That's kinda what it's like. The resistance, the fear, the sheer terror - that's fuel for the fire, in a way. It creates blockages, makes the energy feel more chaotic, more painful. It's like trying to hold your breath underwater. You can do it for a bit, but eventually, you're gonna gasp, and it's gonna be a lot more intense when you do. So yeah, the idea of stopping it? It's a natural reaction, a desperate plea for relief, but it's not a viable strategy.

Why You Can't Just Hit Pause

Look, this isn't some external force that's been imposed on you. It's an internal process. It's you. Or rather, it's your system doing what it's designed to do, what it's been trying to do perhaps for a long time. It's like puberty, but for your soul. Can you stop puberty once it starts? No. You can try to ignore it, you can try to fight the changes, but your body's gonna do what it's gonna do. This is the same principle, just on a different plane. This energy, this intelligence, it's waking up. It's moving. It's clearing out old patterns, old traumas, old beliefs that no longer serve you. And trust me, there's a lot of old junk in there for most of us. It's uncomfortable, it's scary, it feels like chaos, but it's not arbitrary. There's a method to this madness, even if you can't see it right now.

I remember trying to suppress it. I'd try to distract myself, drink too much, work too much, anything to just make the buzzing, the heat, the visions, the emotional tidal waves go away. It never worked. It just made me feel more exhausted, more disconnected, and when the energy eventually broke through, it was with a vengeance. It was like a spring being coiled tighter and tighter, only to release with more force. So, when you ask if you can stop it, I want you to understand that the question itself comes from a place of fear and a lack of understanding of what's actually happening. You're not being attacked; you're being upgraded. It doesn't feel like an upgrade, I know. It feels like you're being dismantled. But sometimes, you gotta break something down to build it back better, right?

What Happens When You Try to Suppress It

Trying to stop a kundalini awakening is like trying to hold a beach ball underwater. You can do it for a while, but it's exhausting, and the moment you let go, it shoots to the surface with incredible force. That's what happens when you try to suppress this energy. You're creating resistance, and resistance creates friction, which creates more heat, more pressure, more distress. You'll probably experience things like:

  • Increased physical symptoms: The tremors might get worse, the heat more intense, the pain more acute. It's like the energy is trying harder to break through the wall you're putting up.
  • Emotional volatility: You're already probably experiencing wild mood swings, but trying to suppress it can increase them. You might feel more irritable, more depressed, more anxious, because you're fighting your own internal process.
  • Mental fog and confusion: Your mind is already probably a chaotic mess, but active suppression can make it even harder to think clearly. You're using so much mental energy to fight, there's not much left for anything else.
  • Worsening of psychological issues: If you have any pre-existing mental health conditions, trying to suppress kundalini can definitely exacerbate them. It's like throwing gasoline on a fire.
  • Feeling more alone and isolated: When you're trying to hide what's happening, or deny it, you're building walls around yourself. This process is already isolating enough; suppression just makes it worse. You feel like you can't talk to anyone, because who would understand? And if they did, you wouldn't want them to know you're fighting something you can't win.

I've seen it happen, and I've lived it. I tried to intellectualize it away, to rationalize it, to categorize it as just anxiety or stress. But the body doesn't lie. The energy doesn't care about your intellectual explanations. It's there. It's moving. And it demands attention. So, the question isn't "how do I stop it?" The real question, the one that will actually help you, is "how do I work with it?"

Working With, Not Against, the Energy

This is where the shift happens. This is where you move from victim to participant. It's not about stopping it, it's about guiding it. It's about learning to surf the wave instead of getting pummeled by it. It's not easy, I won't lie to you. It's the hardest thing you'll ever do. But it's also the most important.

One of the first things you need to do is accept that this is happening. Full stop. No more fighting. No more denial. Just a simple, gut-wrenching acceptance. "Okay, this is my reality now." That acceptance, even if it's grudging, is like opening a valve. It releases some of the pressure. It makes space for something else to happen.

Then, you start to learn. You start to educate yourself. I devoured books during my crisis, desperate for anything that made sense of what I was experiencing. The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) was a lifeline for me. It showed me I wasn't crazy, that others had gone through this. Reading about the experiences of others, learning the terminology - it gave me a approach, a way to understand the seemingly random chaos. It's like getting a map when you're lost in a jungle. You still have to walk, but at least you know which direction you're generally headed.

Practical Steps for Easing the Ride

So, you can't stop it. What can you do? A lot. And these aren't quick fixes, they're practices, lifestyle changes, ways of being that will help you integrate this immense energy. They'll help you flow with it, rather than constantly crashing against it.

  • Grounding, grounding, grounding: I can't say this enough. You're probably feeling like you're floating outside your body, or like your head is in the clouds. You need to get back into your body, into the earth. Walk barefoot on grass. Eat root vegetables. Spend time in nature. Heavy, earthy foods can help. Avoid stimulants like caffeine and sugar as much as you can. They just make the energy more erratic.
  • Simple, gentle movement: Intense exercise might feel like too much right now, but gentle movement is key. Think slow walks, gentle stretching, restorative yoga. Tai Chi or Qigong can be incredibly helpful for moving energy without overstimulating yourself. The goal isn't to burn it off, but to help it flow.
  • Hydration and nutrition: Your body is working overtime. It needs good fuel. Drink plenty of water. Eat whole, unprocessed foods. Your system is detoxing, clearing out old stuff, and it needs support.
  • Rest and sleep: Sleep might be a distant memory, I know. But do everything you can to create a conducive environment for rest. Dark room, no screens before bed, calming rituals. Even if you can't sleep, resting your body and mind is still incredibly valuable.
  • Creative expression: This energy needs an outlet. If you don't give it one, it'll find its own, and it might not be pretty. Write, draw, paint, sing, dance. Don't worry if it's "good." Just let it out. It's a way to process the immense internal shifts happening.
  • Connect with others who understand: This is huge. You need people who won't tell you you're crazy, or try to fix you with platitudes. Online forums, support groups, or even just one trusted friend who's open-minded. Reading books like When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) or The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) can make you feel less alone, too. Bonnie Greenwell's work is gold for this stuff.
  • Seek professional support: I'm not talking about a doctor who's going to tell you it's all in your head and prescribe a bunch of meds that might just numb you. I'm talking about someone who understands spiritual emergence, like a transpersonal therapist or a spiritual counselor who's knowledgeable about these processes. They exist. It might take some searching, but they do. Someone who can hold space for you, validate your experience, and offer guidance without pathologizing you. Sometimes, you need someone to help you sort through the noise. And honestly, it's okay to get help.
  • Mindfulness and gentle awareness: Instead of fighting the sensations, try to observe them. It's not about meditating for hours if you can't sit still. It's about noticing, "Okay, there's heat in my spine. There's a tremor in my hands. There's intense sadness." Just observing without judgment. This creates a tiny bit of distance, a tiny bit of space between you and the intensity, which can be a huge relief.
  • Acceptance of the unknown: This is probably the hardest one. You don't know when it'll end. You don't know what you'll become. You don't know how long the pain will last. This whole thing is a surrender to the mystery. And that's terrifying. But when you stop trying to control every outcome, when you let go of needing to know all the answers right now, some of the tension eases. It really does.

The Long Haul: Patience and Persistence

This isn't a sprint, it's a marathon. And sometimes, it feels like an ultra-marathon through a desert with no water. There will be good days, and there will be absolutely horrific days. There will be moments of clarity and bliss, and moments where you just want to curl up in a ball and disappear. That's okay. All of it is part of the process. You're going through a deep transformation, and that's not neat or tidy. It's messy, it's confusing, it's often agonizing.

You'll probably feel like you're losing your identity. Because you are. The "you" that existed before this started is being shed, layer by painful layer. And that's terrifying. Who will you be on the other side? You don't know. And that's okay. You have to trust that there is an "other side." You have to trust that this process, as awful as it feels right now, is leading you somewhere. Somewhere more authentic, more aligned, more whole. It's not a punishment; it's an initiation. A brutal one, I'll grant you, but an initiation nonetheless.

Remember, the goal isn't to stop the energy, but to integrate it. To allow it to do its work, to clear what needs clearing, to heal what needs healing. It's about becoming a better container for this immense power. It's about expanding your capacity to hold more light, more awareness, more truth. And that takes time. It takes immense courage. And it takes a willingness to face everything you've ever run from.

I'll tell you what, when I was in the thick of it, I thought I was broken beyond repair. I thought I'd lost my mind. I thought I'd never feel normal again. But here I am, on the other side of the worst of it. Still evolving, still learning, but no longer in the grip of that sheer terror. The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) was another book that helped me understand the bigger picture of spiritual emergence and crisis. It showed me that these intense, seemingly pathological experiences are often part of a natural healing process. It's a journey, not a destination. And you're on it. You're not alone. You're doing the hard work. Keep going. Just keep going. One breath at a time. One moment at a time.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
image_alt: Can You Stop a Kundalini Awakening Once It Starts?
